rules.rule_statement: Forming a partnership requires the intent and agreement to carry on a business for profit as co-owners. The agreement may be implied by the conduct of the parties when they have not entered into a written or oral agreement. Although a written agreement is not necessary to form a general partnership, a partnership agreement that cannot be performed in one year is subject to the Statute of Frauds. The specific intent to form a partnership is not required, and the co-owners’ subjective intent not to form a partnership does not prevent the formation of a partnership. Example: A and B decide to sell flowers from A’s garden at the local gardening fair. B secures the space at the fair and A cuts the flowers from the garden and prepares the bouquets for the fair. Neither party intends to form a partnership. However, they verbally agree to split the profits 50-50. A and B have formed a partnership.

Required Intent And Agreement
Forming a partnership requires the intent and agreement to carry on a business for profit as co-owners. The agreement may be implied by the conduct of the parties when they have not entered into a written or oral agreement. Although a written agreement is not necessary to form a general partnership, a partnership agreement that cannot be performed in one year is subject to the Statute of Frauds. The specific intent to form a partnership is not required, and the co-owners’ subjective intent not to form a partnership does not prevent the formation of a partnership. Example: A and B decide to sell flowers from A’s garden at the local gardening fair. B secures the space at the fair and A cuts the flowers from the garden and prepares the bouquets for the fair. Neither party intends to form a partnership. However, they verbally agree to split the profits 50-50. A and B have formed a partnership.

rules.rule_statement: Passive co-ownership of property by itself does not create a partnership. Instead, co-ownership of a business requires the sharing of control over the business. Courts will consider the number of related activities directed toward achieving a business’s end goal when determining whether a partnership exists.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, if a partnership does not exist, a person cannot be liable to a third party as a partner. However, even though a partnership does not exist or someone is not a partner of an established partnership, a purported partner may be liable as a partner. Rule A person who makes or consents to a representation that the person is a partner in an actual or purported partnership is a purported partner (or a partner by estoppel). If a third party suffered damages because of reasonable reliance on the representation, the purported partner is liable as if the purported partner were a partner.

rules.rule_statement: A partnership is a legal entity that is distinct from its partners. A partnership may hold property and can also sue and be sued. An action may be brought against the partnership and any or all of the partners in the same action or in separate actions. Partners are not protected from personal liability for the partnership’s obligations. Additionally, a partner’s knowledge or notice of a fact relating to the partnership is generally immediately imputed to the partnership unless a fraud on the partnership is committed by or with the consent of the partner.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A partner is an agent of the partnership for its business purposes. As an agent, the partner can commit the partnership to binding contracts with third parties. A partner also owes the partnership and the other partners two fiduciary duties—the duty of loyalty and the duty of care. In observing the duties of loyalty and care and in exercising any rights, a partner has the obligation of good faith and fair dealing. The partnership agreement cannot eliminate this obligation, but it can prescribe reasonable standards by which the performance of the obligation is measured.

The partnership agreement cannot eliminate this obligation, but it can prescribe reasonable standards by which the performance of the obligation is measured.

rules.rule_statement: ii) Advancing an interest adverse to the partnership; and iii) Usurping a partnership opportunity or otherwise using partnership property or business to derive a personal benefit, without notifying the partnership. A partnership agreement may not eliminate the duty of loyalty. However, the agreement may identify specific types or categories of activities that do not violate this duty, if not manifestly unreasonable. Example: A real estate partnership agreement may permit partners to retain commissions on partnership property bought and sold. The agreement also may provide a safe harbor regarding a transaction between a partner and the partnership. Under a safe harbor, a certain number or percentage of the other partners—after full disclosure of the material facts—can authorize or ratify the transaction. Example: A partnership agreement may provide that a transaction by a partner that would otherwise constitute a breach of the duty of loyalty is permitted if (1) the material facts of the transaction and the partner’s interest were disclosed to all partners and (2) a majority of the disinterested partners approved or ratified the transaction.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The duties of loyalty and care generally apply only to partners. Dealings between prospective partners in forming a partnership are not subject to these duties, but prospective partners may be subject to the general contractual obligations to deal honestly and without fraud. On a partner’s dissociation from a partnership or on the partnership’s dissolution, these duties do not apply unless the partner is engaged in winding up the partnership business. When a partner or the representative of the last surviving partner is engaged in winding up the partnership business, these duties are generally applicable, except for the noncompete aspect of the duty of loyalty.

rules.rule_statement: The partnership agreement controls a partner’s rights to share in the partnership’s profits and losses. The agreement may specify a percentage for sharing profits that differs from the percentage for sharing losses. Neither profits nor losses are required to be shared on a per capita basis. Example 1: Partners X, Y, and Z form a partnership and execute a written partnership agreement. The agreement provides that Partner X gets 70% of the profits and that Partners Y and Z each get 15% of the profits. The agreement also provides that losses will be shared equally among the partners. The percentages provided in the agreement are controlling.

Dictated By Agreement
The partnership agreement controls a partner’s rights to share in the partnership’s profits and losses. The agreement may specify a percentage for sharing profits that differs from the percentage for sharing losses. Neither profits nor losses are required to be shared on a per capita basis. Example 1: Partners X, Y, and Z form a partnership and execute a written partnership agreement. The agreement provides that Partner X gets 70% of the profits and that Partners Y and Z each get 15% of the profits. The agreement also provides that losses will be shared equally among the partners. The percentages provided in the agreement are controlling.

rules.rule_statement: If there is no agreement or the agreement is silent regarding the division of the profits and losses, each partner is entitled to an equal share of the partnership profits and losses. Example 2: Same facts as Example 1, but the agreement fails to address how profits and losses will be divided. Profits and losses will be shared equally among the partners. When the agreement addresses only the division of partnership profits, partnership losses are shared in the same manner. Example 3: Same facts as Example 1, but the agreement is silent regarding losses. Partner X will be liable for 70% of the losses, and Partners Y and Z will each be liable for 15% of the losses.

When the agreement addresses only the division of partnership profits, partnership losses are shared in the same manner. rules.rule_statement: Rule A partner has the right to transfer his partnership interest to a third party. Transfer of a partnership interest (e.g., assignment) may include his entire interest or only part of it. If the partnership agreement contains a restriction that prohibits the transfer of a partnership interest, then a transfer in violation of this restriction is ineffective regarding a transferee who has notice of the restriction. Transfer of a partnership interest does not automatically trigger the dissolution of the partnership or the partner’s dissociation from the partnership. However, the transfer allows the transferee to seek a judicial order for dissolution of the partnership. The transferor partner retains all rights and duties of a partner in the partnership apart from an interest in the distributions transferred. The partnership is not required to give effect to the transfer until the partnership has notice of the transfer. The transferee has the right to receive distributions from the partnership to which the transferor partner would otherwise have been entitled. In the event of dissolution, the transferee is entitled to an accounting of the distributions, but only for the period beginning from the date of the last accounting agreed to by all of the partners. The transferee is not entitled to participate in the management or conduct of the partnership business, to access partnership records, or to demand other information from the partnership.

rules.rule_statement: A creditor of a partner who has obtained a judgment against the partner may enforce that judgment against the partner’s partnership interest only by obtaining a judicial charging order. The charging order constitutes a lien on the partner’s partnership interest. Available remedies include a court-appointed receiver of the partner’s distributions from the partnership and a court- ordered foreclosure of interest. The purchaser of the interest at a foreclosure sale is treated as a transferee.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A partner has the power to dissociate from the partnership at any time, even if the dissociation is wrongful. For a partnership that is unlimited by time or undertaking, a partner’s dissociation is wrongful only when it is in breach of an express provision of the partnership agreement. For a partnership that is for a definite term or undertaking, the partner’s dissociation is wrongful when, before the expiration of the term or completion of the undertaking, the partner: because of death, bankruptcy, or other circumstances described below; ii) Is expelled by court order; iii) Is a debtor in bankruptcy; or iv) Is not an individual, a trust, or an estate, and the partner willfully dissolved or terminated. A partner who wrongfully dissociates is liable to the partnership and the other partners for damages caused by the dissociation. Wrongful dissociation from a partnership that is unlimited by time or undertaking triggers dissolution. Wrongful dissociation from a partnership that is for a definite term or undertaking also triggers dissolution if a majority of the remaining partners agree to wind up the partnership within 90 days of dissociation. If the partnership does not dissolve and wind up, the wrongfully dissociated partner is not entitled to payment of any portion of the buyout price until the expiration of the term or completion of the undertaking, unless the partner proves to the court that earlier payment will not cause undue hardship to the business of the partnership. A deferred payment must be adequately secured and bear interest.

rules.rule_statement: A partner’s dissociation does not necessarily cause the dissolution of the partnership and the winding up of its business (see II.C.1. “Events Causing Dissolution,” infra). discuss the following effects of dissociation. A dissociated partner generally does not have the right to participate in the management or conduct of the partnership business. A partner’s duty not to compete terminates upon dissociation. The dissociated partner’s other duties of loyalty and care terminate with respect to post-dissociation events, unless the partner participates in winding up the partnership’s business if the partnership itself dissolves. When a partner dissociates from the partnership but the partnership is not dissolved, the partnership must buy out the dissociated partner’s partnership interest.

rules.canonical_name: Partner’S Power To Bind The Partnership
rules.rule_statement: Rule A partner is an agent of the partnership for its business purposes and can contractually bind the partnership when the partner acts with either actual or apparent authority. Crossover Topic: Agency For a more detailed discussion of actual and apparent agency authority, see the Themis Agency outline at § II.B.1. Actual Authority and § II.B.2. Apparent Authority. A partner’s act with actual authority (express or implied) binds the partnership. Even if a partner lacks actual authority, the partner’s act may nevertheless bind the partnership under the principle of apparent authority if the unauthorized act occurred in the ordinary course of apparently carrying on either the partnership business or business of a kind carried on by the partnership. However, the third party with whom the partner was dealing cannot hold the partnership liable when that party knew or received notification that the partner lacked authority. A third party receives notification when the third party is aware of the notification or the notification is delivered to the third party’s place of business or other place for receiving communications. Regarding the transfer of real estate, a third party is regarded as knowing a restriction on a partner’s authority contained in a properly filed statement of authority or dissociation.

rules.rule_statement: A partnership may file a statement of partnership authority. This statement may specify the extent of a partner’s authority to enter into transactions on behalf of the partnership. A purchaser of partnership property (or other person who gives value in a transaction with a partner) may point to a grant of authority contained in a filed statement as conclusive evidence of that authority. There is no requirement that the person relied upon or was aware of the statement at the time of the transaction. However, the person cannot have had actual knowledge that the partner lacked the necessary authority to enter into the transaction. A limitation on a partner’s authority contained in a filed statement generally does not constitute constructive notice to a third party of that limitation. An exception exists regarding a limitation contained in a statement on the authority of a partner to transfer real property held in the name of the partnership. To constitute constructive notice, a certified copy of the statement must be filed in the office for recording transfers of that real property.

rules.rule_statement: Termination of a partnership is a two-step process—dissolution and winding up. When an event occurs that triggers dissolution, this is the first step in the termination of a partnership. Next, the partnership must wind up its business, which is a process that entails liquidating the assets, paying off creditors, and distributing any remaining funds to the partners. The partnership is not terminated until the partnership business is wound up.

rules.rule_statement: Rule In winding up a partnership’s business, creditors have priority over partners to the partnership’s assets. Partnership assets are first applied to discharge partnership obligations to creditors (including partners who are creditors of the partnership) before being distributed to the partners. Unlike the UPA, the RUPA does not subordinate the obligations to partner creditors to those of third-party creditors. However, even partner creditors remain personally liable, as a partner, for unsatisfied debts to outside creditors when partnership assets are insufficient to satisfy all obligations to non-partners. Each partner’s account must be adjusted to reflect the profits and losses that result from the liquidation of the partnership assets. After these adjustments, any partners with a negative account balance must contribute to the partnership the amount necessary to bring the account balance to zero. Then the partnership must make a final liquidating distribution to any partner with a positive account balance.

rules.rule_statement: A limited partnership is a partnership formed by two or more persons that has at least one general partner and at least one limited partner. A limited partner is an investor protected by limited liability who contributes capital to a limited partnership in exchange for a proportionate share of the limited partnership’s profits. A general partner is a partner with the rights, powers, and liabilities of a partner in a partnership without limited partners. Limited partnerships are governed by the Revised Uniform Limited Partnership Act of 1976 with the 1985 amendments (RULPA), which is linked to the RUPA.

rules.source_text:
Limited Partnerships
A limited partnership is a partnership formed by two or more persons that has at least one general partner and at least one limited partner. A limited partner is an investor protected by limited liability who contributes capital to a limited partnership in exchange for a proportionate share of the limited partnership’s profits. A general partner is a partner with the rights, powers, and liabilities of a partner in a partnership without limited partners. Limited partnerships are governed by the Revised Uniform Limited Partnership Act of 1976 with the 1985 amendments (RULPA), which is linked to the RUPA.

rules.rule_statement: Rule To form a limited partnership, a certificate of limited partnership must be filed with the state. The certificate must contain only the name of the limited partnership, its in-state address, the name and address of its in- state agent for service of process, the name and business address of each general partner, and a statement about the duration of the limited partnership. All general partners must sign the certificate. The limited partnership comes into existence on the filing of the certificate unless the certificate specifies a later date. A certificate only needs to substantially comply with statutory requirements to have effect as to third parties. If a certificate of limited partnership is not filed, then the limited partnership is not formed. If a person contributes to a purported limited partnership and erroneously believes in good faith that he has become a limited partner, then he is liable to a third party who transacts business with the purported limited partnership, believing in good faith that the person was a general partner at the time of the transaction. A similar result occurs when a person is mistakenly listed as a general partner in the certificate.

rules.source_text:
Limited Partner’S Derivative Action
A limited partner has the right to bring a derivative action on behalf of the limited partnership.

rules.rule_statement: A limited partner may bring an action if the general partners have refused to do so or if an attempt to cause the general partners to bring such an action is likely to fail. In the complaint, the limited partner is required to detail the effort or explain the reasons for not making the effort.

rules.rule_statement: To bring the action, the limited partner must be a partner at the time of bringing the action as well as at the time of the wrongful transaction, unless the status of partner devolved on him by operation of law or under the terms of the operating agreement.
